one_line_takeaway: A query-only attack that steals and cleanly removes n-gram LLM watermarks without knowing their parameters, dropping detector TPR from ~90% to single digits while preserving text quality and even forging watermarks onto attacker content, exposing a serious robustness gap in red-green-list watermarking.
key_finding: DE-MARK removes the watermark almost completely while barely touching text quality: on Llama3.1-8B (MMW Book Report) TPR@0.1%FPR drops from 89% to 2% and the median p-value rises from 1.10e-6 to 1.78e-1, with GPT score essentially unchanged (94.81 -> 94.63). Across the three open-source models TPR@0.1%FPR falls from the 87-95% range to 2-15%. Watermark stealing recovers the red/green list with over 85% accuracy in both gray-box and black-box settings (gray-box F1 ~86.9). Exploitation forges detectable watermarks onto previously-unwatermarked text (TPR@0.1%FPR 0% -> 93% gray box, 94% black box on MMW Book Report). The attack transfers to a commercial model: on ChatGPT it cuts TPR@0.1%FPR from 89.58% to 25.00%.
core_contribution: DE-MARK is an attack framework that reverse-engineers and removes n-gram (red-green list) LLM watermarks of the Kirchenbauer et al. (2023a) family without prior knowledge of the watermark's parameters. Its central technique is 'random selection probing': the target LLM is prompted to choose uniformly at random between two candidate tokens/phrases, so that in an unwatermarked model the log-ratio of the two token probabilities (the 'relative probability ratio' R_ij) is ~0, but under an n-gram watermark it collapses to approximately +delta or -delta when the two tokens straddle the red/green boundary. From this signal the paper builds five algorithms that (1) compute the pairwise relative-ratio matrix, (2) turn it into per-token red/green scores, (3) identify the prefix n-gram length h, (4) estimate the watermark strength delta as an unbiased average of extreme ratios (Theorem 4.1), and (5) recover the green list. Given the recovered (delta-hat, R-hat, G-hat), the paper gives a distribution-preserving removal rule (Eq. 2) with a proven total-variation bound between the post-removal distribution P_R and the original unwatermarked distribution P (Theorem 4.2), and an 'exploitation' mode that forges the stolen watermark onto content produced by a separate attacker LLM. The key claimed advantage over the prior watermark-stealing method (Jovanovic et al., 2024) is that DE-MARK needs neither knowledge of the hash function nor a paraphrase tool, and therefore preserves the original LM distribution rather than distorting it.
threat_model: Adversary attacks a watermarked LLM service. Detector is fully private (No-access / D0) and the attacker has no access to non-watermarked base responses (B0). The watermark's parameters (prefix length h, strength delta, red/green lists) are unknown. Two capability tiers: gray-box (L1) where the attacker can read top-k token log-probabilities (e.g. ChatGPT exposes top-20 logprobs) and black-box (L0) where only sampled outputs are available and probabilities must be estimated from repeated queries. Watermark removal specifically requires the L1 (gray-box) tier because the TV distance bound depends on token probabilities; stealing and exploitation work in both L0 and L1. Attacker goals: (a) steal the watermarking rule, (b) strip the watermark so the detector fails, and (c) exploit the stolen rule to stamp a valid watermark onto arbitrary (e.g. harmful) content generated by an attacker-controlled model, framing the victim.
limitations: 1) Scope is restricted to n-gram / red-green-list watermarks of the Kirchenbauer family; distortion-free schemes (Aaronson Gumbel, Christ et al., Kuditipudi et al.) and semantic/unigram-robust variants are out of scope. 2) Watermark removal requires gray-box top-k log-probability access (L1); the authors explicitly exclude the pure black-box removal case because the TV bound cannot then be guaranteed. 3) delta estimation degrades for small delta where noise dominates (the paper leans on delta=2 being the common/HuggingFace default). 4) No direct quantitative head-to-head against competing stealing methods: the paper argues Jovanovic et al. (2024) needs the hash function and a paraphraser, and states concurrent work (Pang et al. 2024, Zhang et al. 2024) uses incompatible settings, so the tables compare only watermarked vs. +DE-MARK rather than attacker-vs-attacker. 5) The exploitation/removal both assume the underlying watermark really is n-gram-structured; robustness to unknown or mixed watermark families is not evaluated. 6) Small evaluation corpora (~100 prompts per dataset).

datasets_used: Dolly Creative Writing (Conover et al., 2023), ~100 prompts
MMW Book Report (Piet et al., 2023), ~100 prompts
quantitative_results: Watermark removal, Llama3.1-8B, MMW Book Report: TPR@0.1%FPR 89% -> 2% (0.01%: 75% -> 1%; 0.001%: 65% -> 1%); median p-value 1.10e-6 -> 1.78e-1; GPT score 94.81 -> 94.63
Watermark removal, Llama3.1-8B, Dolly CW: TPR@0.1%FPR 92% -> 10%; median p-value 1.88e-7 -> 1.78e-1; GPT score 92.97 -> 92.88
Watermark removal, Mistral-7B, MMW Book Report: TPR@0.1%FPR 95% -> 15%; median p-value 1.51e-6 -> 2.48e-2; GPT score 94.53 -> 94.46
Watermark removal, Llama3.2-3B, MMW Book Report: TPR@0.1%FPR 91% -> 6%; median p-value 3.43e-7 -> 8.29e-2; GPT score 94.22 -> 93.98
Watermark exploitation, MMW Book Report: No-watermark TPR@0.1%FPR 0% -> 93% (gray box) / 94% (black box); median p-value 4.54e-2 -> 1.10e-6 (gray) / 5.72e-6 (black)
Watermark exploitation, Dolly CW: No-watermark TPR@0.1%FPR 0% -> 79% (gray box) / 84% (black box)
Red-green list detection (Table 3): gray box F1 86.93 / Acc 87.07 (MMW Book Report); black box F1 86.09 / Acc 86.15; all settings >85% accuracy
ChatGPT case study (Table 4): baseline TPR@0.1%FPR 89.58% -> 25.00% with DE-MARK (0.01%: 80.21% -> 12.50%; 0.001%: 63.54% -> 6.25%); median p-value 7.61e-7 -> 6.78e-3
Prefix-length identification: consistency rate drops below beta=0.8 exactly at the true h for h in {3,7} across all three models (gray and black box)
delta estimation accurate for delta in {1.0,1.5,2.0,3.0,4.0}, especially at larger delta; less accurate for small delta
baselines_compared: Watermarked model without removal vs. +DE-MARK (primary in-table comparison, Tables 1, 4)
Gray-box (L1, top-20 logprobs) vs. black-box (L0, 10-100 samples) variants of DE-MARK

Qualitative-only contrast with prior watermark-stealing work Jovanovic et al. (2024) (needs hash function + paraphraser); concurrent Pang et al. (2024) and Zhang et al. (2024) noted as not directly comparable due to different settings
